---
title: "Day 6 — Exercise: Hello Yew"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-copy: true
    code-overflow: wrap
    highlight-style: github
---

## Goal

Get the day-6 template running on your machine: a Yew frontend served by [trunk](https://trunkrs.dev) on `http://127.0.0.1:8080`, talking to an axum backend on `http://127.0.0.1:3000`. Then make a small change to the frontend and watch trunk hot-reload the browser.

By the end of this exercise you will have **seen WebAssembly in action**: the very same `*.rs` files we have been writing all week, compiled to `.wasm`, running directly in your browser tab.

## Background

A Yew app is a Rust program that compiles to the `wasm32-unknown-unknown` target instead of native machine code. The browser loads the resulting `.wasm` blob (alongside a tiny JavaScript shim that `wasm-bindgen` generates) and runs it inside the same sandbox as any other web page.

[Trunk](https://trunkrs.dev) is the build tool that ties this together: one command (`trunk serve`) compiles the Rust to WASM, generates the JavaScript glue, bundles them with the `index.html`, serves the result over HTTP, **and** watches your source files so it rebuilds and reloads the page whenever you save.

The other half of the story lives in `backend/`: a normal native [axum](https://docs.rs/axum/) HTTP server that we will fetch data from in [exercise 3](03-fetch-from-backend.qmd). Today we just confirm it runs.

## One-off setup

If you have not already:

```bash
rustup target add wasm32-unknown-unknown
cargo install trunk
```

Both take a few minutes the first time and only need to run once on your machine. `wasm-bindgen-cli` will be auto-downloaded by trunk on the first build.

## The exercise

```bash
cd day6/ex-sequence-inspector
```

You will need **two terminal windows** open at the same time.

### Terminal 1 — start the backend

```bash
cargo run -p backend
```

After a minute or so of compiling axum & friends, you should see:

```
backend listening on http://127.0.0.1:3000
```

Leave this running. Open a browser to <http://127.0.0.1:3000/api/samples>. You should see a JSON list of sample records — five entries, with `id` and `name` fields but no sequence.

### Terminal 2 — start the frontend

```bash
cd frontend
trunk serve
```

First run: takes a couple of minutes (it is compiling Yew, gloo, and a few hundred small crates to WebAssembly). Subsequent runs are much faster. Watch for:

```
server listening at http://127.0.0.1:8080
```

Open <http://127.0.0.1:8080>. You should see:

> # Sequence inspector
>
> Hello from Yew and WebAssembly!
>
> Edit frontend/src/main.rs and save — trunk reloads the page for you.

If that worked, **the rest of day 6 is downhill**. The big "is my toolchain alive" question is settled.

### Make your first change

Open `frontend/src/main.rs`. Find the `html!` block:

```rust
html! {
    <div>
        <h1>{ "Sequence inspector" }</h1>
        <p>{ "Hello from Yew and WebAssembly!" }</p>
        <p>{ "Edit frontend/src/main.rs and save — trunk reloads the page for you." }</p>
    </div>
}
```

Make any small change — replace the heading with your name, add a third `<p>` element, anything. **Save the file**. Watch your browser tab.

You should see trunk re-build in the terminal (a few seconds for an incremental change), then the page reloads automatically and shows your edit.

That feedback loop is what you will use for the rest of the week. Keep both terminals open and just edit code.

## Hints

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 1 — anatomy of the `html!` macro"}
The [`html!`](https://docs.rs/yew/0.23.0/yew/macro.html.html) macro lets you write HTML-like syntax with [Rust expressions embedded in `{ }`](https://yew.rs/docs/concepts/html). A few rules:

- Every tag must be closed (`<br/>`, not `<br>`).
- Text content goes inside `{ }`. Bare text like `<p>Hi</p>` will not compile — write `<p>{ "Hi" }</p>`.
- You can put any Rust expression inside `{ }`: `<p>{ 1 + 1 }</p>` renders "2"; `<p>{ format!("Hello {}", name) }</p>` renders an interpolated string.
- Multiple top-level elements need to be wrapped in a fragment (`<></>`) or a parent element (`<div></div>`).
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 2 — trunk did not reload the page"}
Make sure you actually saved the file (`Ctrl+S` / `Cmd+S`). Some editors with autosave will save when the window loses focus, but VS Code's default is to save explicitly.

If trunk shows a compile error in its terminal, the page will not reload until you fix it. The error is the same Rust compiler error you would get from `cargo build` — read it the same way.

Hard refresh the browser tab (`Ctrl+Shift+R` / `Cmd+Shift+R`) if you suspect something stuck.
:::

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" title="Hint 3 — port already in use"}
If trunk fails with `address already in use (os error 98)`, either:

- You already have trunk running in another terminal — keep one, close the other.
- Something else is on port 8080. Stop it, or start trunk on a different port: `trunk serve --port 8081`.

Same story for the backend on 3000.
:::

## Solution

::: {.callout-note collapse="true" title="A worked example diff"}
There is no single "right" change for this exercise — anything that gets reflected in the browser counts. As an example, the heading could become:

```rust
html! {
    <div>
        <h1>{ "Sequence inspector — by Alice" }</h1>
        <p>{ "Compiled to WebAssembly from Rust." }</p>
    </div>
}
```

If trunk recompiled and the browser shows your change, you have hit every learning outcome for today.
:::

## Where this leads

- [Exercise 2](02-reactive-stats.qmd) — make the page interactive: paste a DNA sequence into a `<textarea>` and watch the GC content update on every keystroke. No server required yet.
- The backend's `/api/samples` endpoint will not be used until [exercise 3](03-fetch-from-backend.qmd). It is already running in your other terminal because we want you to see, today, that the two halves are independent processes — and that the frontend can talk to the backend via the `/api/...` proxy configured in `frontend/Trunk.toml`.
